---
author: "Vera Okisheva, Raphael Klöckner and Tristan Muno"
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: theme.scss
    slideNumber: true
    footer:  TBCI   -- {{< meta date >}} --  The Muslim Penalty
    logo:  ./images/uma_ss.png
preview-links: true
---

## The Muslim Penalty

### Experimental Evidence from 25 European Countries

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}}<br> {{< meta date >}}

# Part I: Study Design

## Research Context {.smaller}

- Re-analysis of data from @hahm2024divided; a large-scale study of affective polarization across 25 EU countries
- Paper compares behavioral bias across social divides — partisanship vs. other identities (e.g. religion, class, nationality)
- Central idea:
  - Individuals categorize others into partisan in-groups and out-groups
  → in-group favoritism and out-group discrimination
- Authors assume partisanship to be the dominant divide; stronger than other social identities
- We build on this by focusing on one dimension: the role of religion


## Experimental Design I {.smaller}

- Respondents participate in an online survey experiment
- In each round, they interact with a fictitious co-player (“Player 2”)
- Player 2 is described by a profile with multiple attributes:
  - party affiliation, religion, nationality, class, age, gender
- All attributes are randomly assigned (conjoint design)
- Each respondent completes:
  - 3 dictator games and 3 trust games, each with a new randomized profile

## Experimental Design II

![Player 2 Profile Example.<br>*Note:* Reproduced from @hahm2024divided, Figure 1.](https://static.cambridge.org/binary/version/id/urn:cambridge.org:id:binary:20240123150926403-0273:S0003055423000266:S0003055423000266_fig1.png)

---

## Randomisation of Religion Attribute 

{{< embed code/01_test.qmd#fig-randomization >}}

# Part II: Findings from @hahm2024divided

## Effects of Identity Attributes on Allocations to Player 2

![Effects of Identity Attributes on Allocations to Player 2.<br> *Note:* Reproduced from @hahm2024divided, Figure 2.](https://static.cambridge.org/binary/version/id/urn:cambridge.org:id:binary:20240123150926403-0273:S0003055423000266:S0003055423000266_fig2.png)


## {.smaller}

**The Problem of Binary Coding**

-   @hahm2024divided operationalize religious distance as a binary: **In-group** vs. **Out-group** across 4 categories (Catholic, Protestant, Muslim, and No Religion). This assumes out-group homogeneity.
-   In a European context, the social distance between a Catholic and a Protestant is significantly smaller than the distance between a Christian and a Muslim.

<br>

**Why it Matters**

-   **Dilution:** Low bias between Christian denominations 'dilutes' the average effect of the religious     out-group.
-   **Underestimation:** The paper might underestimate the true power of religious divide by masking the acute hostility toward specific out-groups.
-   Is this religious effect driven primarily by bias against the **Muslim** group?

## Methodological Trap {.smaller}

Average Marginal Component Effects (AMCE) present effects relative to a baseline, which can be misleading when analyzing subgroup preferences.

<br>

#### The Reference Category Problem
-   @hahm2024divided use of a binary 'In-group/Out-group' allows them to avoid this
-   No meaningful reference category in this case [see @leeper2020measuring]
-   'Muslim' might be an In-group for a Muslim respondent but an Out-group for a Catholic. A single AMCE coefficient for 'Muslim' would average these opposing directions, washing out the effect.


# Part III: Proposed Extensions

## Exploratory Results{.smaller}

{{< embed code/01_test.qmd#fig-tokens-to-muslim-profiles >}}

## Path 1: Comparative Analysis

- Leverage cross-national data and analyse Conditional Average Treatment Effect of Muslim Attribute in Games on Token Allocation, focusing on cross-national variation

## Path 2: Individual-Level Covariate Analysis

- What individual level (observed) covariates predict stronger Muslim bias?
- Apply methods learned in this course for honest inference and subgroup analysis in conjoint experiments


# Thank you for your attention!

---

## References
